figures.caption: Figure 2. Illustration of the interpolation procedure used to infer ?̂? from the map (3.8). Here the green curve represents the lab-side property integral 𝐻𝐿 and the blue curve represents its flight-side counterpart evaluated on an arbitrary grid. The mapping procedure follows a continuous path from the laboratory state to the flight state: starting at 𝑔𝐿 , we compute the lab integral 𝐻𝐿 , scale it to the flight target H𝐿 = 𝑒𝐾0𝐻𝐿 , and find the corresponding flight temperature ?̂? through the inversion of the 𝐻𝐹 curve.

Figure 3. Thermophysical property profiles in laboratory and flight conditions and the corresponding boundarylayer temperature profiles for three test cases. Panels (a–d), (f–i), and (k–n) show the laboratory and flight laws for ℓ0, 𝜒, 𝑅𝜌, and𝐶𝑝 , respectively; panels (e), (j), and (o) compare the mapped temperature profile ?̂?(𝜂) with the laboratory 𝑔𝐿 (𝜂𝐿) and flight 𝑔𝐹 (𝜂𝐹 ) profiles. Row 1: 𝑇𝐿𝑤 = 100 K, 𝑇𝐹𝑤 = 1000 K, 𝑇𝐿𝑒 = 2935 K, 𝑇𝐹𝑒 = 3103 K. Row 2: 𝑇𝐿𝑤 = 100 K, 𝑇𝐹𝑤 = 200 K, 𝑇𝐿𝑒 = 2935 K, 𝑇𝐹𝑒 = 4165 K. Row 3: 𝑇𝐿𝑤 = 100 K, 𝑇𝐹𝑤 = 1000 K, 𝑇𝐿𝑒 = 2935 K, 𝑇𝐹𝑒 = 8586 K.
Figure 4. Comparison of transformed ?̂?𝑝 obtained from the 𝜒-based reparametrization of temperature for the two cases presented in the rows 2 (panel (a) here) and 3 (panel (b) here) of figure 3.
